paragraphs.text: - SRD‑46 contains matched Co2+ and Co3+ stability data for several amine ligands: ammonia (NH3, ligand_10103), ethylenediamine (en, ligand_7029), diethylenetriamine (dien, ligand_7212), triethylenetetramine (trien, ligand_7224), tris(2‑aminoethyl)amine (tren, ligand_7345), and aminopolycarboxylates such as EDTA (ligand_6277), NTA (ligand_6165), DTPA (ligand_6356). It also has both oxidation states for aromatic diimines (2,2′‑bipyridine, ligand_8156; 1,10‑phenanthroline, ligand_8191). These were verified via SRD‑46 metal and ligand catalogs for Co2+ (metal_33) and Co3+ (metal_34).

paragraphs.text: Based on that dataset context and well‑established cobalt chemistry (and consistent with the SRD‑46 coverage pattern), the qualitative comparison is:

claims.text: Based on that dataset context and well‑established cobalt chemistry (and consistent with the SRD‑46 coverage pattern), the qualitative comparison is:

grounded_claims.evidence_snippet: Filler span: no useful database-groundable information.

paragraphs.text: - For any ligand studied with both oxidation states, β(Co3+) » β(Co2+). The Co3+ ion is smaller, higher charge, and for typical octahedral N‑donor fields adopts low‑spin d6 (t2g6, large LFSE), whereas Co2+ is usually high‑spin d7 with lower LFSE and much greater lability. In practice this yields very large positive Δlogβ = logβ[Co(III)] − logβ[Co(II)] across amines, with the gap increasing as donor set and chelate number increase.

claims.text: - For any ligand studied with both oxidation states,
this yields very large positive Δlogβ
with the gap increasing as donor set and chelate number increase.

Spin state/electron configuration not given in SRD-46 tables here; low‑spin d6 for Co(III) in typical octahedral N‑donor fields is standard inorganic chemistry.

LFSE magnitudes are not listed in SRD-46 outputs; the statement reflects standard ligand field theory for low‑spin d6 Co(III).

Complex lability/inertness is not given in SRD-46 tables; Co(II) being more labile than Co(III) is well-known (Water exchange kinetics/Inert Co(III)).

No numerical series in tool results to verify the trend; chelate/donor-set dependence of larger Δlogβ for Co(III) vs Co(II) is standard chelate effect reasoning.

paragraphs.text: - Monodentate amines (e.g., NH3) already show β(Co3+) » β(Co2+). Moving to chelators (en < dien < trien < tren; aminopolycarboxylates like EDTA, DTPA), both oxidation states gain from the chelate effect, but the relative gain is larger for Co3+.

claims.text: both oxidation states gain from the chelate effect
the relative gain is larger for Co3+.

paragraphs.text:
Prediction for a new polydentate amine ligand:
- Co3+: Expect very high cumulative stability constants (large logβ1 and especially high overall β for the fully chelated 1:1 complex), low‑spin, kinetically inert complexes; in practice these often resist substitution and may even form only under oxidizing conditions (strong macrocycles can assist CoII→CoIII).
- Co2+: Expect moderate stability (smaller logβ and overall β), high‑spin and significantly more labile complexes; stepwise formation constants will be lower and dissociation faster relative to the Co3+ analogs.

claims.text: Prediction for a new polydentate amine ligand:
Expect very high cumulative stability constants
in practice these often resist substitution
may even form only under oxidizing conditions
stepwise formation constants will be lower
dissociation faster relative to the Co3+ analogs
